title: Psalms 79
subtitle: The Nations Have Invaded Your Inheritance

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “They Have Laid Jerusalem in Ruins”

heb.paragraph: "O God, the nations have invaded your inheritance (naḥălâ); they have defiled your holy temple, they have reduced Jerusalem to rubble" (v.1). A communal lament over Jerusalem’s destruction (either 586 BC or another devastation). The dead are given to birds and beasts (v.2), blood poured out like water (v.3). Israel is scorned by neighbours (v.4). "How long, LORD? How long will your anger burn?" (v.5).

notes.note: "They have defiled your holy temple; they have reduced Jerusalem to rubble. The dead bodies of your servants have been given to birds; blood has been poured out like water." The devastation is total: temple, city, corpses, blood.
"How long, LORD? Will your jealousy burn like fire?" The "how long" cry — the default question of suffering Israel.

refs.note: Jeremiah's prophecy of Jerusalem's desolation parallels the destruction this psalm laments.
The historical account of Nebuzaradan burning the temple and breaking down walls fulfills this imagery.
Nations trampling the holy city for 42 months echoes the psalm's invasion lament.

hist.context: Psalm 79 is a communal lament over Jerusalem's destruction, likely by Babylon in 586 BC. The description of corpses left unburied, blood poured out, and becoming 'a reproach to our neighbors' (vv.2-4) indicates total catastrophe. The cry 'how long, LORD?' (v.5) is the characteristic lament question when suffering seems endless.

sections.header: Verses 8–13 — “Help Us, God Our Saviour, for the Glory of Your Name”

heb.paragraph: "Help us, God our Saviour, for the glory of your name (shēm)" (v.9). The appeal shifts: rescue us not for OUR sake but for YOUR name’s reputation. "Why should the nations say, ‘Where is their God?’" (v.10). God’s honour is at stake. "We your people, the sheep of your pasture, will praise you forever" (v.13).

notes.note: "Help us for the glory of your name. Why should the nations say, ‘Where is their God?’" The argument from divine reputation: God’s honour is bound to Israel’s fate.

"Then we your people, the sheep of your pasture, will praise you forever; from generation to generation we will proclaim your praise." The vow: rescue produces praise that lasts through generations. Suffering now; worship forever.

refs.note: God enduring vessels of wrath to make known his glory develops the psalm's concern for divine reputation.
Acting for the sake of his holy name profaned among nations echoes the psalm's plea.
Martyrs crying 'how long until you avenge our blood?' directly parallels verse 10.

hist.context: The appeal 'for the glory of your name' (v.9) argues that God's reputation is at stake — if Israel remains crushed, nations will mock Israel's God. The taunting question 'where is their God?' (v.10) demands divine response. The request for sevenfold vengeance (v.12) follows ancient Near Eastern conventions of proportional (or disproportional) justice.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Why should the nations say, ‘Where is their God?’" The argument from divine reputation: God’s honour is bound to Israel’s fate…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVHelp us, God our Savior, for the glory of your name; deliver us and forgive our sins for your name’s sake.ESVHelp us, O God of our salvation, for the glory of your name; deliver us, and atone for our sins, for your name\u2019s sake!NIVHow long, Lord? Will you be angry forever? How long will your jealousy burn like fire?ESVHow long, O LORD? Will you be angry forever? Will your jealousy burn like fire?

rec.text: ""They have defiled your holy temple; they have reduced Jerusalem to rubble. The dead bodies of your servants have been given to birds; blood has been poured out like water." The devastation is total: …"

rows.label: The Devastation — 79:1–7
rows.text: "They have reduced Jerusalem to rubble." Dead to birds. Blood like water. "How long?"

hebtext.note: "O God, the nations have invaded your inheritance (naḥălâ); they have defiled your holy temple, they have reduced Jerusalem to rubble" (v.1). A communal lament over Jerusalem’s destruction (either 586 BC or another devastation). The dead are given to birds and beasts (v.2), blood poured out like water (v.3). Israel is scorned by neighbours (v.4). "How long, LORD? How long will your anger burn?" (v.5).

"Help us, God our Saviour, for the glory of your name (shēm)" (v.9). The appeal shifts: rescue us not for OUR sake but for YOUR name’s reputation. "Why should the nations say, ‘Where is their God?’" (v.10). God’s honour is at stake. "We your people, the sheep of your pasture, will praise you forever" (v.13).

themes.note: Judgment: Jerusalem’s destruction. Covenant: "your inheritance" invaded. Justice: the nations’ violence. The appeal to God’s name and reputation.
